revision.text:
The Myoshin-ji school of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] [[Buddhism]] is the largest school in [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]]. This particular school contains within it more than three thousand temples throughout [[Japan]], along with ninteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the venerable Zen Master Kanzan-Egen Zenji (1277-1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Onin War]] in [[1467]], however, many of them have since been rebuilt.
A notable difference between this and other schools of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] is that the Myoshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established [[Koans]] for the sake of testing one's stage of [[enlightenment]]. Rather the Myoshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor [[Koans]] to a student's needs and background, though this method is occasionally met with a certain degree of hostility from the other schools in so far as it diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of [[Koans]].
==External links== *[http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism]
{{zen-stub}}

[[Image:MyoshinjiTaizoin1.jpg|thumb|Taizo-in at Myoshin-ji]] '''Myōshin-ji''' is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The Myoshin-ji school of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] [[Buddhism]] is the largest school in [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]]. This particular school contains within it more than three thousand temples throughout [[Japan]], along with ninteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the venerable Zen Master Kanzan-Egen Zenji (1277-1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Onin War]] in [[1467]], however, many of them have since been rebuilt.
A notable difference between this and other schools of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] is that the Myoshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established [[Koans]] for the sake of testing one's stage of [[enlightenment]]. Rather the Myoshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor [[Koans]] to a student's needs and background, though this method is occasionally met with a certain degree of hostility from the other schools in so far as it diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of [[Koans]].
==External links== *[http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism]
{{zen-stub}} {{commons|Myoshin-ji}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The Myōshin-ji school of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] [[Buddhism]] is the largest school in Rinzai Zen. This particular school contains within it more than three thousand temples throughout Japan, along with nineteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (1277-1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]; however, many of them have been rebuilt. A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (concept)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' == School == *[[Hanazono University]] ==See also== *[[Goto Zuigan]] *[[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] ==External links== *[http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site *[http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] *[http://www.shunkoin.com/ Shunkōin] (a sub-temple of Myōshin-ji) {{buddhism2}} {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} {{zen-stub}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myôshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]]

''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. The Myōshin-ji school of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] [[Buddhism]] is the largest school in Rinzai Zen. This particular school contains within it more than three thousand{{fact}} temples throughout Japan, along with nineteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (1277-1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]; however, many of them have been rebuilt. A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butusden - Built in 1827. ** Hatto - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hojo - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hojo - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** Kyozo - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindo - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hatto File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hojo File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Sub-temples== * [[Taizō-in]] * [[Daishin-in]] * [[Tōrin-in]] * [[Shunkō-in]] == School == * [[Hanazono University]] == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Goto Zuigan]] * [[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. == External links == * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] * [http://www.shunkoin.com/ Shunkōin] (a sub-temple of Myōshin-ji) {{buddhism2}} {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] {{zen-stub}} [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

The Myōshin-ji school of [[Rinzai]] [[Zen]] [[Buddhism]] is the largest school in Rinzai Zen. This particular school contains within it more than three thousand{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, along with nineteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (1277–1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]; however, many of them have been rebuilt. A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. The Myōshin-ji school of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]] is the largest school in Rinzai Zen. This particular school contains within it more than three thousand{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, along with nineteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (1277–1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]; however, many of them have been rebuilt. A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' The gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) Daitoku-ji ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not part of the main complex include: * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == * [[Hanazono University]] == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Goto Zuigan]] * [[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]], and head temple of the associated branch of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]]. The Myōshin-ji school is the largest school in Rinzai Zen, contains within it more than three thousand{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, along with nineteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (1277–1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]; however, many of them have been rebuilt, and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{ja icon}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{ja icon}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Access== The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 46 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} in the main complex. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) Daitoku-ji ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not part of the main complex include: * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == * [[Hanazono University]] == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Goto Zuigan]] * [[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Goto Zuigan]] * [[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]], and head temple of the associated branch of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]]. The Myōshin-ji school is the largest school in Rinzai Zen, contains within it more than three thousand{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, along with nineteen [[monasteries]]. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (1277–1360). Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]; however, many of them have been rebuilt, and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{ja icon}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{ja icon}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Access== The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 46 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} in the main complex. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not part of the main complex include: * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Goto Zuigan]] * [[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]], and head temple of the associated branch of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]]. The Myōshin-ji school is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, contains within it about three thousand five hundred{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples. It also has nineteen associated [[monasteries]]. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|[[Emperor Hanazono]], who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formally a palace for the [[Emperor Hanazono]]. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the place to found the temple; the district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential [[Ōtōkan]] lineage. Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily build during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),[http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{ja icon}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{ja icon}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 46 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} in the main complex. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not part of the main complex include: * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * [[Kanzan Egen]] 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * [[Gudō Toshoku]] (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Goto Zuigan]] * [[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]], and head temple of the associated branch of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]]. The Myōshin-ji school is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, contains within it about three thousand five hundred{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples. It also has nineteen associated [[monasteries]]. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|[[Emperor Hanazono]], who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formally a palace for the [[Emperor Hanazono]]. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the place to found the temple; the district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential [[Ōtōkan]] lineage. Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily build during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),[http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{ja icon}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{ja icon}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Access== The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 46 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} in the main complex. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not part of the main complex include: * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * [[Kanzan Egen]] 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * [[Gudō Toshoku]] (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 48 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple, of which 38 are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples the main complex include: * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * [[Kanzan Egen]] 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * [[Gudō Toshoku]] (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]], and head temple of the associated branch of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]]. The Myōshin-ji school is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about three thousand five hundred{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[monasteries]], of the total of forty monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|[[Emperor Hanazono]], who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formally a palace for the [[Emperor Hanazono]]. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the place to found the temple; the district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in the year 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential [[Ōtōkan]] lineage. Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily build during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),[http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{ja icon}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{ja icon}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Access== The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 48 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple, of which 38 are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples the main complex include: * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * [[Kanzan Egen]] 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * [[Gudō Toshoku]] (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Access== The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 40-odd {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples the main complex include: * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * [[Kanzan Egen]] 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * [[Gudō Toshoku]] (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | img = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', [[Butsuden]], ''Hattō'' | landscape = | denomination = | founded = | closed = | founder = | teacher = | director = | roshi = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = | country = | phone = | website = http://www.myoshin.com/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]], and head temple of the associated branch of [[Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[Japanese Zen|Zen]] [[Buddhism]]. The Myōshin-ji school is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about three thousand five hundred{{citation needed|date=January 2011}} temples throughout Japan, together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[monasteries]], of the total of forty monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|[[Emperor Hanazono]], who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formally a palace for the [[Emperor Hanazono]]. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the palace to found the temple; the district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in 1342 by the [[Zen master]] [[Kanzan Egen]] (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential [[Ōtōkan]] lineage. Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the [[Ōnin War]] in [[1467]]. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily build during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),[http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally-designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=401&item_id=1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''[[kōan]]'' for the sake of testing one's stage of [[Enlightenment (spiritual)|enlightenment]]. Rather the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.'' ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{ja icon}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{ja icon}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north-south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, starting parallel to the main buildings, then continuing north, flanked by veering slight, and ending at the north gate. There is also an east-west path leading east from the main buildings (starting at in the west at Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north-south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past [[Keishun-in]], then terminates in the east-west just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths. These paths are all lined with sub-temples, generally with a single entrance. ==Buildings== *Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu ==Access== The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to north; there are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. At the south there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}} (hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the north and south gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji station and Ryōan-ji station on the [[Randen]] line, near north gate * [[Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|Hanazono Station]] on the JR [[Sagano Line]], near the south gate ==Sub-temples== There are 40-odd {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{ja icon}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|[[Taizō-in]]|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|[[Shunkō-in]]|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, [[shukubō]] * {{Nihongo|[[Keishun-in]]|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) ;Seasonal openings * [[Tōrin-in]] – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round [[shukubō]] and [[shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * [[Ryōan-ji]] – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples the main complex include: * [[Enshō-ji (Nara)]] * [[Sōken-ji]] * [[Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)]] == School == [[Hanazono University]], the Rinzai university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * [[Kanzan Egen]] 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * [[Gudō Toshoku]] (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan]] * For an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture, see the [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, ISBN 978-90-04-16557-1 * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''[[The Japan Times]],'' March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe == External links == {{commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshin.com/ Myōshin-ji] Official site * [http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{buddhism2}} {{coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Myoshin-Ji}} [[Category:1342 establishments]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]] [[de:Myōshin-ji]] [[fr:Myōshin-ji]] [[ja:妙心寺]] [[pnb:میوشن-جی]] [[ru:Мёсин-дзи]] [[vi:Chùa Myōshin]] [[zh:妙心寺]]
