[[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | native_name = | image = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, ''Hattō'' | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | deity = | country = | functional_status = | website = http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ | founded_by = | year_completed = }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, which serves as the head temple of the associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai|1=Rinzai school}} {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Zen|1=Japanese Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. The Myōshin-ji School is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about 3,400 temples throughout Japan,{{cite journal |last1=李 |first1=賀敏 |title=日本臨濟宗名剎--妙心寺 |journal=Voice of Dharma |date=1999 |issue=3 |pages=28 |publisher=Fa Yin Bian Ji Bu |issn=1004-2636}} together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[:en:Monasteries|Monasteries]], of the total of forty Rinzai monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|{{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}, who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formerly a palace for the {{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the palace to found the temple. The district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in 1342 by the {{ill|Zen master|en|Zen master|WD=Q1998748|lt=Zen master|1=Zen master}} {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential {{ill|Ōtōkan|en|Ōtōkan|WD=Q8081781|lt=Ōtōkan|1=Ōtōkan}} lineage. As with most Zen temples in Japan, in addition to the name Myōshin-ji, the temple complex also has a "mountain name," harkening back to the period in China when most Chinese Zen temples were located in the mountains. Myōshin-ji's mountain name is Shōbōzan (正法山), or "Mountain of the True {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}."{{Cite web |date=2008 |title=Myoshin-ji Temple / History |url=https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/english/history/ |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji Temple}} Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1467. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily built during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),{{Cite web |url=http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |title=The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism |access-date=2012-06-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303235836/http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |archive-date=2016-03-03 |url-status=dead }} and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple's bell, known as ''Okikicho'', is the oldest-known example of a Buddhist ''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}'' ("temple bell") in Japan, as well as being the oldest bell in the world still in use. It was cast in 698.{{cite book |first=Thomas |last=Rossing |title=Science of Percussion Instruments |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0FCUngEACAAJ&q=thomas+rossing+science+of+percussion+instruments |year=2000 |publisher=World Scientific |isbn=978-981-02-4158-2 |page=179}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''{{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan|1=Koan}}'' for the sake of testing one's stage of {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. Rather, the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.''{{Cite web |title=Myoshin-ji {{!}} Japan Experience |url=https://www.japan-experience.com/all-about-japan/kyoto/temples-shrines/myoshin-ji |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji}} ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{in lang|ja}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{in lang|ja}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented when walking the grounds. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis that extends north from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north–south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, which starts parallel to the main buildings, and then continues north, through a slight curve, and ends at the north gate. There is also an east–west path leading east from the main buildings (starting in the west at the sub-temple Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at the sub-temple Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north–south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past {{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}, and then terminates just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths which are all lined with sub-temples, generally each with a single entrance. ==Buildings== * Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to the north. There are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. On the south side of Myōshin-ji there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}}, hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the North and South Gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji Station and {{ill|Ryōanji station|en|Ryōanji station|WD=Q11679223|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōanji station}} Station on the {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|en|Keifuku Electric Railroad|WD=Q3537126|lt=Randen|1=Keifuku Electric Railroad}} line, near the North Gate * {{ill|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|en|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|WD=Q1044135|lt=Hanazono Station|1=Hanazono Station (Kyoto)}} on the JR {{ill|Sagano Line|en|Sagano Line|WD=Q1073011|lt=Sagano Line|1=Sagano Line}}, near the South Gate ==Sub-temples== There are more than 40 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[:en:塔頭|塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{in lang|ja}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Taizō-in|en|Taizō-in|WD=Q3514106|lt=Taizō-in|1=Taizō-in}}|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Shunkō-in|en|Shunkō-in|WD=Q669494|lt=Shunkō-in|1=Shunkō-in}}|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) * [https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/worship/keidai/323 Reiun-in] (霊雲院) -- pocket rock garden ;Seasonal openings * {{ill|Tōrin-in|en|Tōrin-in|WD=Q3547079|lt=Tōrin-in|1=Tōrin-in}} – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} and [[:en:shōjin-ryōri|shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * {{ill|Ryōan-ji|en|Ryōan-ji|WD=Q587371|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōan-ji}} – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples of the main complex include: * {{ill|Enshō-ji (Nara)|en|Enshō-ji (Nara)|WD=Q2671301|lt=Enshō-ji (Nara)|1=Enshō-ji (Nara)}} * {{ill|Sōken-ji|en|Sōken-ji|WD=Q3511694|lt=Sōken-ji|1=Sōken-ji}} * {{ill|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|en|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3576268|lt=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|1=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q7506113|lt=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|1=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Erin-ji |en|Erin-ji |WD=Q6430158|lt= Erin-ji (Yamanashi)|1=Erin-ji}} * {{ill|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|en|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|WD=Q17866872|lt=Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|1=Bairin-ji (Kurume)}} == School == {{ill|Hanazono University|en|Hanazono University|WD=Q5646966|lt=Hanazono University|1=Hanazono University}}, the Rinzai-School university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * {{ill|Gudō Toshoku|en|Gudō Toshoku|WD=Q5614720|lt=Gudō Toshoku|1=Gudō Toshoku}} (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[:en:Goto Zuigan|Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|en|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|WD=Q109420052|lt=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|1=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, {{ISBN|978-90-04-16557-1}} * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''{{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|WD=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}'', March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe * Buswell, Robert Jr; Lopez, Donald S. Jr., eds. (2013). Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, p. 559. {{ISBN|9780691157863}}. == External links == {{Commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ Myōshin-ji] Official site (includes English section) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061205041931/http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} }} [[en:Myōshin-ji]] [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{draft categories| [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{wikidata link|Q376288}} [[Category:1340s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1342 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | native_name = | image = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, ''Hattō'' | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | deity = | country = | functional_status = | website = http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ | founded_by = | year_completed = }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, which serves as the head temple of the associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai|1=Rinzai school}} {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Zen|1=Japanese Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. The Myōshin-ji School is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about 3,400 temples throughout Japan,{{cite journal |last1=李 |first1=賀敏 |title=日本臨濟宗名剎--妙心寺 |journal=Voice of Dharma |date=1999 |issue=3 |pages=28 |publisher=Fa Yin Bian Ji Bu |issn=1004-2636}} together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[:en:Monasteries|Monasteries]], of the total of forty Rinzai monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|{{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}, who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formerly a palace for the {{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the palace to found the temple. The district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in 1342 by the {{ill|Zen master|en|Zen master|WD=Q1998748|lt=Zen master|1=Zen master}} {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential {{ill|Ōtōkan|en|Ōtōkan|WD=Q8081781|lt=Ōtōkan|1=Ōtōkan}} lineage. As with most Zen temples in Japan, in addition to the name Myōshin-ji, the temple complex also has a "mountain name," harkening back to the period in China when most Chinese Zen temples were located in the mountains. Myōshin-ji's mountain name is Shōbōzan (正法山), or "Mountain of the True {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}."{{Cite web |date=2008 |title=Myoshin-ji Temple / History |url=https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/english/history/ |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji Temple}} Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1467. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily built during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),{{Cite web |url=http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |title=The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism |access-date=2012-06-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303235836/http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |archive-date=2016-03-03 |url-status=dead }} and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple's bell, known as ''Okikicho'', is the oldest-known example of a Buddhist ''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}'' ("temple bell") in Japan, as well as being the oldest bell in the world still in use. It was cast in 698.{{cite book |first=Thomas |last=Rossing |title=Science of Percussion Instruments |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0FCUngEACAAJ&q=thomas+rossing+science+of+percussion+instruments |year=2000 |publisher=World Scientific |isbn=978-981-02-4158-2 |page=179}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''{{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan|1=Koan}}'' for the sake of testing one's stage of {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. Rather, the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.''{{Cite web |title=Myoshin-ji {{!}} Japan Experience |url=https://www.japan-experience.com/all-about-japan/kyoto/temples-shrines/myoshin-ji |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji}} ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{in lang|ja}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{in lang|ja}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented when walking the grounds. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis that extends north from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north–south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, which starts parallel to the main buildings, and then continues north, through a slight curve, and ends at the north gate. There is also an east–west path leading east from the main buildings (starting in the west at the sub-temple Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at the sub-temple Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north–south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past {{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}, and then terminates just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths which are all lined with sub-temples, generally each with a single entrance. ==Buildings== * Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu == Directions == The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to the north. There are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. On the south side of Myōshin-ji there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}}, hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the North and South Gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji Station and {{ill|Ryōanji station|en|Ryōanji station|WD=Q11679223|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōanji station}} Station on the {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|en|Keifuku Electric Railroad|WD=Q3537126|lt=Randen|1=Keifuku Electric Railroad}} line, near the North Gate * {{ill|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|en|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|WD=Q1044135|lt=Hanazono Station|1=Hanazono Station (Kyoto)}} on the JR {{ill|Sagano Line|en|Sagano Line|WD=Q1073011|lt=Sagano Line|1=Sagano Line}}, near the South Gate ==Sub-temples== There are more than 40 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[:en:塔頭|塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{in lang|ja}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Taizō-in|en|Taizō-in|WD=Q3514106|lt=Taizō-in|1=Taizō-in}}|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Shunkō-in|en|Shunkō-in|WD=Q669494|lt=Shunkō-in|1=Shunkō-in}}|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) * [https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/worship/keidai/323 Reiun-in] (霊雲院) -- pocket rock garden ;Seasonal openings * {{ill|Tōrin-in|en|Tōrin-in|WD=Q3547079|lt=Tōrin-in|1=Tōrin-in}} – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} and [[:en:shōjin-ryōri|shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * {{ill|Ryōan-ji|en|Ryōan-ji|WD=Q587371|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōan-ji}} – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples of the main complex include: * {{ill|Enshō-ji (Nara)|en|Enshō-ji (Nara)|WD=Q2671301|lt=Enshō-ji (Nara)|1=Enshō-ji (Nara)}} * {{ill|Sōken-ji|en|Sōken-ji|WD=Q3511694|lt=Sōken-ji|1=Sōken-ji}} * {{ill|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|en|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3576268|lt=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|1=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q7506113|lt=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|1=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Erin-ji |en|Erin-ji |WD=Q6430158|lt= Erin-ji (Yamanashi)|1=Erin-ji}} * {{ill|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|en|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|WD=Q17866872|lt=Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|1=Bairin-ji (Kurume)}} == School == {{ill|Hanazono University|en|Hanazono University|WD=Q5646966|lt=Hanazono University|1=Hanazono University}}, the Rinzai-School university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * {{ill|Gudō Toshoku|en|Gudō Toshoku|WD=Q5614720|lt=Gudō Toshoku|1=Gudō Toshoku}} (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[:en:Goto Zuigan|Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|en|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|WD=Q109420052|lt=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|1=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, {{ISBN|978-90-04-16557-1}} * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''{{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|WD=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}'', March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe * Buswell, Robert Jr; Lopez, Donald S. Jr., eds. (2013). Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, p. 559. {{ISBN|9780691157863}}. == External links == {{Commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ Myōshin-ji] Official site (includes English section) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061205041931/http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} }} [[en:Myōshin-ji]] [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{draft categories| [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{wikidata link|Q376288}} [[Category:1340s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1342 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

妙心寺 | native_name = | image = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, ''Hattō'' | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | deity = | country = | functional_status = | website = http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ | founded_by = | year_completed = }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, which serves as the head temple of the associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai|1=Rinzai school}} {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Zen|1=Japanese Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. The Myōshin-ji School is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about 3,400 temples throughout Japan,{{cite journal |last1=李 |first1=賀敏 |title=日本臨濟宗名剎--妙心寺 |journal=Voice of Dharma |date=1999 |issue=3 |pages=28 |publisher=Fa Yin Bian Ji Bu |issn=1004-2636}} together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[:en:Monasteries|Monasteries]], of the total of forty Rinzai monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|{{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}, who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formerly a palace for the {{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the palace to found the temple. The district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in 1342 by the {{ill|Zen master|en|Zen master|WD=Q1998748|lt=Zen master|1=Zen master}} {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential {{ill|Ōtōkan|en|Ōtōkan|WD=Q8081781|lt=Ōtōkan|1=Ōtōkan}} lineage. As with most Zen temples in Japan, in addition to the name Myōshin-ji, the temple complex also has a "mountain name," harkening back to the period in China when most Chinese Zen temples were located in the mountains. Myōshin-ji's mountain name is Shōbōzan (正法山), or "Mountain of the True {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}."{{Cite web |date=2008 |title=Myoshin-ji Temple / History |url=https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/english/history/ |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji Temple}} Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1467. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily built during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),{{Cite web |url=http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |title=The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism |access-date=2012-06-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303235836/http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |archive-date=2016-03-03 |url-status=dead }} and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple's bell, known as ''Okikicho'', is the oldest-known example of a Buddhist ''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}'' ("temple bell") in Japan, as well as being the oldest bell in the world still in use. It was cast in 698.{{cite book |first=Thomas |last=Rossing |title=Science of Percussion Instruments |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0FCUngEACAAJ&q=thomas+rossing+science+of+percussion+instruments |year=2000 |publisher=World Scientific |isbn=978-981-02-4158-2 |page=179}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''{{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan|1=Koan}}'' for the sake of testing one's stage of {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. Rather, the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.''{{Cite web |title=Myoshin-ji {{!}} Japan Experience |url=https://www.japan-experience.com/all-about-japan/kyoto/temples-shrines/myoshin-ji |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji}} ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{in lang|ja}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{in lang|ja}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented when walking the grounds. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis that extends north from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north–south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, which starts parallel to the main buildings, and then continues north, through a slight curve, and ends at the north gate. There is also an east–west path leading east from the main buildings (starting in the west at the sub-temple Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at the sub-temple Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north–south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past {{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}, and then terminates just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths which are all lined with sub-temples, generally each with a single entrance. ==Buildings== * Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu == Directions == The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to the north. There are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. On the south side of Myōshin-ji there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}}, hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the North and South Gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji Station and {{ill|Ryōanji station|en|Ryōanji station|WD=Q11679223|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōanji station}} Station on the {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|en|Keifuku Electric Railroad|WD=Q3537126|lt=Randen|1=Keifuku Electric Railroad}} line, near the North Gate * {{ill|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|en|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|WD=Q1044135|lt=Hanazono Station|1=Hanazono Station (Kyoto)}} on the JR {{ill|Sagano Line|en|Sagano Line|WD=Q1073011|lt=Sagano Line|1=Sagano Line}}, near the South Gate ==Sub-temples== There are more than 40 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[:en:塔頭|塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{in lang|ja}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Taizō-in|en|Taizō-in|WD=Q3514106|lt=Taizō-in|1=Taizō-in}}|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Shunkō-in|en|Shunkō-in|WD=Q669494|lt=Shunkō-in|1=Shunkō-in}}|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) * [https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/worship/keidai/323 Reiun-in] (霊雲院) -- pocket rock garden ;Seasonal openings * {{ill|Tōrin-in|en|Tōrin-in|WD=Q3547079|lt=Tōrin-in|1=Tōrin-in}} – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} and [[:en:shōjin-ryōri|shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * {{ill|Ryōan-ji|en|Ryōan-ji|WD=Q587371|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōan-ji}} – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples of the main complex include: * {{ill|Enshō-ji (Nara)|en|Enshō-ji (Nara)|WD=Q2671301|lt=Enshō-ji (Nara)|1=Enshō-ji (Nara)}} * {{ill|Sōken-ji|en|Sōken-ji|WD=Q3511694|lt=Sōken-ji|1=Sōken-ji}} * {{ill|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|en|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3576268|lt=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|1=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q7506113|lt=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|1=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Erin-ji |en|Erin-ji |WD=Q6430158|lt= Erin-ji (Yamanashi)|1=Erin-ji}} * {{ill|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|en|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|WD=Q17866872|lt=Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|1=Bairin-ji (Kurume)}} == School == {{ill|Hanazono University|en|Hanazono University|WD=Q5646966|lt=Hanazono University|1=Hanazono University}}, the Rinzai-School university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * {{ill|Gudō Toshoku|en|Gudō Toshoku|WD=Q5614720|lt=Gudō Toshoku|1=Gudō Toshoku}} (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[:en:Goto Zuigan|Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|en|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|WD=Q109420052|lt=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|1=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, {{ISBN|978-90-04-16557-1}} * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''{{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|WD=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}'', March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe * Buswell, Robert Jr; Lopez, Donald S. Jr., eds. (2013). Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, p. 559. {{ISBN|9780691157863}}. == External links == {{Commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ Myōshin-ji] Official site (includes English section) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061205041931/http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} }} [[en:Myōshin-ji]] [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{draft categories| [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{wikidata link|Q376288}} [[Category:1340s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1342 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

妙心寺 | native_name = | image = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, ''Hattō'' | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | deity = | country = | functional_status = | website = http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ | founded_by = | year_completed = }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, which serves as the head temple of the associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai|1=Rinzai school}} {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|WD=Q189424|lt=Zen|1=Japanese Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. The Myōshin-ji School is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about 3,400 temples throughout Japan,{{cite journal |last1=李 |first1=賀敏 |title=日本臨濟宗名剎--妙心寺 |journal=Voice of Dharma |date=1999 |issue=3 |pages=28 |publisher=Fa Yin Bian Ji Bu |issn=1004-2636}} together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[:en:Monasteries|Monasteries]], of the total of forty Rinzai monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|{{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}, who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formerly a palace for the {{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|WD=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the palace to found the temple. The district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in 1342 by the {{ill|Zen master|en|Zen master|WD=Q1998748|lt=Zen master|1=Zen master}} {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential {{ill|Ōtōkan|en|Ōtōkan|WD=Q8081781|lt=Ōtōkan|1=Ōtōkan}} lineage. As with most Zen temples in Japan, in addition to the name Myōshin-ji, the temple complex also has a "mountain name," harkening back to the period in China when most Chinese Zen temples were located in the mountains. Myōshin-ji's mountain name is Shōbōzan (正法山), or "Mountain of the True {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}."{{Cite web |date=2008 |title=Myoshin-ji Temple / History |url=https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/english/history/ |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji Temple}} Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1467. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily built during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),{{Cite web |url=http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |title=The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism |access-date=2012-06-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303235836/http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |archive-date=2016-03-03 |url-status=dead }} and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple's bell, known as ''Okikicho'', is the oldest-known example of a Buddhist ''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|WD=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}'' ("temple bell") in Japan, as well as being the oldest bell in the world still in use. It was cast in 698.{{cite book |first=Thomas |last=Rossing |title=Science of Percussion Instruments |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0FCUngEACAAJ&q=thomas+rossing+science+of+percussion+instruments |year=2000 |publisher=World Scientific |isbn=978-981-02-4158-2 |page=179}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''{{ill|Koan|en|Koan|WD=Q1231764|lt=Koan|1=Koan}}'' for the sake of testing one's stage of {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. Rather, the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.''{{Cite web |title=Myoshin-ji {{!}} Japan Experience |url=https://www.japan-experience.com/all-about-japan/kyoto/temples-shrines/myoshin-ji |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji}} ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{in lang|ja}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{in lang|ja}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented when walking the grounds. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis that extends north from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north–south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, which starts parallel to the main buildings, and then continues north, through a slight curve, and ends at the north gate. There is also an east–west path leading east from the main buildings (starting in the west at the sub-temple Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at the sub-temple Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north–south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past {{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}, and then terminates just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths which are all lined with sub-temples, generally each with a single entrance. ==Buildings== * Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. File:Myoshin-ji 3.JPG|Sanmon File:Myoshinji01s1920.jpg|Butsuden and Hattō File:Myoshinji02n1800.jpg|Dai-hōjō File:Myoshinji08s5s4272.jpg|Yokushitsu == Directions == The complex is located between {{Nihongo|[[Ichijō Street]]|一条通|ichijō-dōri|first street}} to the north and {{Nihongo||下立売通|shimotachiuri-dōri}} to the south, and can be entered from the north or south. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to the north. There are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. On the south side of Myōshin-ji there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}}, hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the North and South Gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji Station and {{ill|Ryōanji station|en|Ryōanji station|WD=Q11679223|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōanji station}} Station on the {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|en|Keifuku Electric Railroad|WD=Q3537126|lt=Randen|1=Keifuku Electric Railroad}} line, near the North Gate * {{ill|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|en|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|WD=Q1044135|lt=Hanazono Station|1=Hanazono Station (Kyoto)}} on the JR {{ill|Sagano Line|en|Sagano Line|WD=Q1073011|lt=Sagano Line|1=Sagano Line}}, near the South Gate ==Sub-temples== There are more than 40 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[:en:塔頭|塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{in lang|ja}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Taizō-in|en|Taizō-in|WD=Q3514106|lt=Taizō-in|1=Taizō-in}}|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Shunkō-in|en|Shunkō-in|WD=Q669494|lt=Shunkō-in|1=Shunkō-in}}|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|WD=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) * [https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/worship/keidai/323 Reiun-in] (霊雲院) -- pocket rock garden ;Seasonal openings * {{ill|Tōrin-in|en|Tōrin-in|WD=Q3547079|lt=Tōrin-in|1=Tōrin-in}} – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} and [[:en:shōjin-ryōri|shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * {{ill|Ryōan-ji|en|Ryōan-ji|WD=Q587371|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōan-ji}} – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples of the main complex include: * {{ill|Enshō-ji (Nara)|en|Enshō-ji (Nara)|WD=Q2671301|lt=Enshō-ji (Nara)|1=Enshō-ji (Nara)}} * {{ill|Sōken-ji|en|Sōken-ji|WD=Q3511694|lt=Sōken-ji|1=Sōken-ji}} * {{ill|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|en|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3576268|lt=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|1=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q7506113|lt=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|1=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Erin-ji |en|Erin-ji |WD=Q6430158|lt= Erin-ji (Yamanashi)|1=Erin-ji}} * {{ill|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|en|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|WD=Q17866872|lt=Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|1=Bairin-ji (Kurume)}} == School == {{ill|Hanazono University|en|Hanazono University|WD=Q5646966|lt=Hanazono University|1=Hanazono University}}, the Rinzai-School university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|WD=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * {{ill|Gudō Toshoku|en|Gudō Toshoku|WD=Q5614720|lt=Gudō Toshoku|1=Gudō Toshoku}} (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[:en:Goto Zuigan|Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|en|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|WD=Q109420052|lt=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|1=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] [[ca:Myōshin-ji]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Myoshinji]][[de:Myōshin-ji]][[en:Myōshin-ji]][[es:Myōshin-ji]][[fa:معبد میوشین]][[fr:Myōshin-ji]][[hu:Mjósindzsi]][[id:Myōshin-ji]][[ja:妙心寺]][[my:မယောရှင်းဂျိ]][[pl:Myōshin-ji]][[pnb:میوشن-جی]][[ru:Мёсин-дзи]][[vi:Myōshin-ji]][[zh:妙心寺]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Myōshin-ji
妙心寺 | native_name = | image = Myoshinji-M9707.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Main buildings. Left to right:
''[[Sanmon]]'', {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}, ''Hattō'' | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | deity = | country = | functional_status = | website = http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ | founded_by = | year_completed = }} {{Nihongo|'''Myōshin-ji'''|妙心寺|''Myōshin-ji''}} is a temple complex in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, which serves as the head temple of the associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai|1=Rinzai school}} {{ill|Japanese Zen|en|Japanese Zen|qid=Q189424|lt=Zen|1=Japanese Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. The Myōshin-ji School is by far the largest school in Rinzai Zen, approximately as big as the other thirteen branches combined: it contains within it about 3,400 temples throughout Japan,{{cite journal |last1=李 |first1=賀敏 |title=日本臨濟宗名剎--妙心寺 |journal=Voice of Dharma |date=1999 |issue=3 |pages=28 |publisher=Fa Yin Bian Ji Bu |issn=1004-2636}} together with a handful overseas, of the approximately six thousand total Rinzai temples, and also has nineteen associated [[:en:Monasteries|Monasteries]], of the total of forty Rinzai monasteries and one nunnery. ==History== [[File:Portrait of Emperor Hanazono.jpg|thumb|right|{{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|qid=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}, who sponsored the foundation of Myōshin-ji, after taking the tonsure]] The grounds of the temple were formerly a palace for the {{ill|Emperor Hanazono|en|Emperor Hanazono|qid=Q440503|lt=Emperor Hanazono|1=Emperor Hanazono}}. Hanazono abdicated in 1318 and took the tonsure (became a monk) in 1335, and in 1342 donated the palace to found the temple. The district and many places in the area are named "Hanazono" in his honor. The head temple was founded in 1342 by the {{ill|Zen master|en|Zen master|qid=Q1998748|lt=Zen master|1=Zen master}} {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|qid=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} (関山慧玄, 1277–1360), third patriarch in the influential {{ill|Ōtōkan|en|Ōtōkan|qid=Q8081781|lt=Ōtōkan|1=Ōtōkan}} lineage. As with most Zen temples in Japan, in addition to the name Myōshin-ji, the temple complex also has a "mountain name," harkening back to the period in China when most Chinese Zen temples were located in the mountains. Myōshin-ji's mountain name is Shōbōzan (正法山), or "Mountain of the True {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}."{{Cite web |date=2008 |title=Myoshin-ji Temple / History |url=https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/english/history/ |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji Temple}} Nearly all of the buildings were destroyed in the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1467. However, many of them have been rebuilt, initially under the leadership of Sekko-Soshin Zenji (1408–1486), the sixth patriarch. The present buildings were primarily built during the following 150 years (late 15th through early 17th century),{{Cite web |url=http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |title=The Head Temples of Zen Buddhism |access-date=2012-06-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303235836/http://www.jikyu-an.com/zen_temples.html |archive-date=2016-03-03 |url-status=dead }} and today the gardens of Myōshin-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]] and [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1672 |title=妙心寺庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple's bell, known as ''Okikicho'', is the oldest-known example of a Buddhist ''{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|qid=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}'' ("temple bell") in Japan, as well as being the oldest bell in the world still in use. It was cast in 698.{{cite book |first=Thomas |last=Rossing |title=Science of Percussion Instruments |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0FCUngEACAAJ&q=thomas+rossing+science+of+percussion+instruments |year=2000 |publisher=World Scientific |isbn=978-981-02-4158-2 |page=179}} == School == A difference between this and other schools of Rinzai Zen is that the Myōshin-ji school does not necessarily follow the set of established ''{{ill|Koan|en|Koan|qid=Q1231764|lt=Koan|1=Koan}}'' for the sake of testing one's stage of {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|qid=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. Rather, the Myōshin-ji school allows the master to specifically tailor ''kōan'' to a student's needs and background. This method diverges from the traditionally accepted canon of ''kōan.''{{Cite web |title=Myoshin-ji {{!}} Japan Experience |url=https://www.japan-experience.com/all-about-japan/kyoto/temples-shrines/myoshin-ji |access-date=October 2, 2023 |website=Myoshin-ji}} ==Layout== {{external media |image1=[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/images/img_map.png 妙心寺山内図] "Myōshin-ji temple map" {{in lang|ja}}[http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/guide/index.html 境内のご案内] "Precincts guide" {{in lang|ja}} }} The complex is quite sprawling, and features a number of winding paths flanked by high walls, so it is easy to become disoriented when walking the grounds. As is usual in Japanese temple construction, the main buildings are located on the axis that extends north from the south gate, in the south-west quadrant of the complex. There is a main north–south path connecting the north gate and the south gate, which starts parallel to the main buildings, and then continues north, through a slight curve, and ends at the north gate. There is also an east–west path leading east from the main buildings (starting in the west at the sub-temple Tenju-in, passing between the hattō and butsuden, then ending in the east, after a curve, at the sub-temple Tōrin-in). In addition to the direct north–south path, there is a longer path that proceeds east from the north gate, winds past {{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|qid=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}, and then terminates just south of [[Daishin-in]]. In addition to these main routes, there are a number of side paths which are all lined with sub-temples, generally each with a single entrance. ==Buildings== * Important Cultural Property of Japan ** Chokushimon - Built in 1610. ** Sanmon - Built in 1599. ** Butsuden - Built in 1827. ** Hattō - Built in 1656. ** Dai-hōjō - Built in 1654. ** Kuri - Built in 1653. ** Sho-hōjō - Built in 1603. ** Yokushitsu - Built in 1656. ** [[Kyōzō]] - Built in 1673. ** Minamimon - Built in 1610. ** Kitamon - Built in 1610. ** Genkan - Built in 1654. ** Shindō - Built in 1656. There are two main gates: one to the south, and one to the north. There are also side entrances that do not pass through a gate. On the south side of Myōshin-ji there is a large gate that is not open, as it is a {{Nihongo||勅使門|chokushi-mon|imperial envoy gate}}, hence only opened for imperial envoys), but next to it is the {{Nihongo|main south gate|南総門|minami-sōmon}}. Just inside both the North and South Gates are small information booths, providing maps and directions. A number of train stations are located nearby: * Myōshin-ji Station and {{ill|Ryōanji station|en|Ryōanji station|qid=Q11679223|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōanji station}} Station on the {{ill|Keifuku Electric Railroad|en|Keifuku Electric Railroad|qid=Q3537126|lt=Randen|1=Keifuku Electric Railroad}} line, near the North Gate * {{ill|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|en|Hanazono Station (Kyoto)|qid=Q1044135|lt=Hanazono Station|1=Hanazono Station (Kyoto)}} on the JR {{ill|Sagano Line|en|Sagano Line|qid=Q1073011|lt=Sagano Line|1=Sagano Line}}, near the South Gate ==Sub-temples== There are more than 40 {{Nihongo|sub-temples|[[:en:塔頭|塔頭]]|tatchū}} of the main temple (Myōshin-ji states 46, but lists 49), of which 30-odd are within the grounds of the main complex, and 10 are in the surrounding area. These include:[http://kyoto.gp1st.com/500/ent13.html 妙心寺塔頭] "Myōshin-ji sub-temples" {{in lang|ja}} ;Within the grounds ;Open year-round * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Taizō-in|en|Taizō-in|qid=Q3514106|lt=Taizō-in|1=Taizō-in}}|退蔵院}} – most noted sub-temple, garden and ink paintings * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Shunkō-in|en|Shunkō-in|qid=Q669494|lt=Shunkō-in|1=Shunkō-in}}|春光院}} – connections with Christianity and with Buddhist philosophy, meditation in English * {{Nihongo|[[Daishin-in]]|大心院}} – rock garden {{nihongo|"aum garden"|阿吽の庭|aun no niwa}}, {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|qid=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Keishun-in|en|Keishun-in|qid=Q11536768|lt=Keishun-in|1=Keishun-in}}|桂春院}} – tea garden (matcha tea served) * [https://www.myoshinji.or.jp/worship/keidai/323 Reiun-in] (霊雲院) -- pocket rock garden ;Seasonal openings * {{ill|Tōrin-in|en|Tōrin-in|qid=Q3547079|lt=Tōrin-in|1=Tōrin-in}} – seasonal openings (parts of January, July, and October), year-round {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|qid=Q11455614|lt=Shukubo|1=Shukubo}} and [[:en:shōjin-ryōri|shōjin-ryōri]] * [[Daihō-in]] 大法院 – spring and autumn openings (new leaves and autumn leaves: early April through early May, and November, respectively) ;Limited admission (conditions apply) * [[Daiyū-in]] 大雄院 ;Closed to public (Others) ;Off the grounds ;Open to the public * {{ill|Ryōan-ji|en|Ryōan-ji|qid=Q587371|lt=Ryōan-ji|1=Ryōan-ji}} – short walk away, world-famous rock garden, UNESCO World Heritage Site ;Limited admission * [[Eshō-in]] 慧照院 – just south of main grounds, limited admission; seasonal opening for camellia garden in late April * [[Seigen-in]] 西源院 – just south of Ryōan-ji, limited admission ;Closed to the public (Others) ==Affiliated temples== Temples in the Myōshin-ji school, but not sub-temples of the main complex include: * {{ill|Enshō-ji (Nara)|en|Enshō-ji (Nara)|qid=Q2671301|lt=Enshō-ji (Nara)|1=Enshō-ji (Nara)}} * {{ill|Sōken-ji|en|Sōken-ji|qid=Q3511694|lt=Sōken-ji|1=Sōken-ji}} * {{ill|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|en|Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|qid=Q3576268|lt=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)|1=Zuiryū-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|qid=Q7506113|lt=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)|1=Shōgen-ji (Gifu)}} * {{ill|Erin-ji |en|Erin-ji |qid=Q6430158|lt= Erin-ji (Yamanashi)|1=Erin-ji}} * {{ill|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|en|Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|qid=Q17866872|lt=Bairin-ji (Kurume)#Arima clan cemetery|1=Bairin-ji (Kurume)}} == School == {{ill|Hanazono University|en|Hanazono University|qid=Q5646966|lt=Hanazono University|1=Hanazono University}}, the Rinzai-School university, was established by Myōshin-ji in 1872, and is located some distance to the southeast of the temple complex. In the southeast corner of the square plot of the temple complex proper is the associated Hanazono High School, just across a small river. == Abbots == Abbots have included: * {{ill|Kanzan Egen|en|Kanzan Egen|qid=Q10809938|lt=Kanzan Egen|1=Kanzan Egen}} 關山慧玄 (1277–1360), founder and first abbot * ? ... ? * {{ill|Gudō Toshoku|en|Gudō Toshoku|qid=Q5614720|lt=Gudō Toshoku|1=Gudō Toshoku}} (1577–1661), thrice abbot * ? ... ? * [[:en:Goto Zuigan|Goto Zuigan]] (1879–1965) == See also == * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|en|Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|qid=Q109420052|lt=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan|1=Ichibata Yakushi Kyodan}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== * "Japanese Rinzai Zen Buddhism. Myoshinji, a living religion" Jørn Borup, Brill, {{ISBN|978-90-04-16557-1}} * "[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fe20020328gt.html Gardens For All: Myoshinji: Zen gardens wondrous to behold, and not]", ''{{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}'', March 28, 2002, by Gerard Taaffe * Buswell, Robert Jr; Lopez, Donald S. Jr., eds. (2013). Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press, p. 559. {{ISBN|9780691157863}}. == External links == {{Commons|Myoshin-ji}} * [http://www.myoshinji.or.jp/ Myōshin-ji] Official site (includes English section) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061205041931/http://web.kyoto-inet.or.jp/org/jikyu-an/zen_temples.html Head Temples of Zen Buddhism] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Coord|35|01|23|N|135|43|13|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} }} [[en:Myōshin-ji]] [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{draft categories| [[en:Myōshin-ji]] {{wikidata link|Q376288}} [[Category:1340s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1342 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zen gardens]]
